ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for wheat cracker; SNOMED CT coverage for processed snack foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of wheat crackers is approximately 30 g (about 5–6 individual crackers), providing roughly 142 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Wheat Cracker — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for wheat cracker (cr_id F11080080); all core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Crackers and Crispbreads — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry for this specific HPB wheat cracker product. GI of 67 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat crackers / plain crackers made from refined wheat flour (GI range 65–70 reported for similar products). Serving size 30 g; available carbohydrate per serving = 62.69 × 0.30 = 18.81 g; GL = (67 × 18.81) / 100 = 12.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 327 mg/100 g and estimated saturated fat ~7 g/100 g contribute to blood pressure elevation and cardiovascular strain.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit wheat cracker intake to a small portion (≤30 g) and choose low-sodium alternatives (<120 mg/100 g) to avoid cumulative sodium loading across the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate content (62.7 g/100 g) with moderate GI (~67) and low dietary fibre (3.94 g/100 g) produces a significant postprandial glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit wheat crackers; if consumed, restrict to ≤15 g per occasion and pair with a protein source (e.g., cheese or nut butter) to attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~7 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g cardiovascular threshold, and the sodium content (327 mg/100 g) adds further cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose wholegrain, low-saturated-fat crackers (saturated fat <3 g/100 g, sodium <200 mg/100 g) and limit portion size to minimise LDL-raising saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~7 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; refined carbohydrate and added sugar promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid wheat crackers with palm oil or partially hydrogenated fats and choose wholegrain, olive-oil-based alternatives with sugar <5 g/100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wheat cracker is a crispy, baked snack biscuit made primarily from refined wheat flour, vegetable oil, salt, and sugar. It belongs to the biscuits, cookies and crackers subgroup within the snacks food category. The texture is characteristically dry and brittle, with a lightly salted, mildly sweet flavour profile. Wheat crackers are widely consumed as a standalone snack or paired with dips, cheese, or spreads. Per 100 g, they provide 472 kcal, 8.21 g protein, 21.09 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~7 g), and 62.69 g carbohydrate with 9.24 g sugar and 3.94 g dietary fibre. Sodium content is 327 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is approximately 67 (medium GI), making them a moderate-to-high glycaemic food. Clinically, wheat crackers are unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to their high refined carbohydrate load and moderate GI. The sodium and saturated fat content raise concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The estimated saturated fat exceeds the threshold relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. Calcium content is notable at 192 mg/100 g and iron at 3.14 mg/100 g, likely from fortification. This food is vegetarian, vegan-friendly, and halal-suitable but contains gluten and is not appropriate for coeliac or gluten-sensitive individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in wheat crackers?
Are wheat crackers high in sodium?
What is the GI of wheat crackers?
Are wheat crackers suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080080) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at ~7 g/100 g based on typical wheat cracker fat composition; this single field is an estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable refined wheat cracker products. All other values are exact from the approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this wheat cracker entry are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11080080) as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g of 7.0 g/100 g is an estimate based on typical fat composition of refined wheat flour crackers using vegetable shortening or palm oil; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB source and should be verified against product label data. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (8.21), fat_g (21.09), and carbohydrate_g (62.69) are all non-zero. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is an international generic snack product, so no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain wheat crackers made from refined flour; GL per 30 g serving = (67 × 18.81) / 100 = 12.6.
